intro: Airship and Batch both show up under the push in app messaging category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Airship uses subscription from $9, while Batch runs on undisclosed pricing with free tier. Lock-in is low for Airship and medium for Batch. Transparency lands at 2/5 versus 4/5. Airship fits teams working on mobile app engagement, while Batch is a closer match when the job is transactional + marketing push with behavioral segmentation SDK. Worth noting: Airship is explicitly not for web-only products without mobile; Batch is explicitly not for teams needing email, SMS, and in-app in one mature suite. The honest trade-off: Airship trades off on web-only products without mobile; Batch trades off on mobile-centric — web push support is secondary. On the plus side, Airship highlights mobile app engagement, while Batch points to strong EU/GDPR posture with data residency. Airship's documentation also calls out user lifecycle messaging. Batch similarly notes reliable push delivery with decent segmentation.
quick_take: Airship is for mobile app engagement; Batch is for transactional + marketing push with behavioral; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Airship if your project is mobile app engagement, a subscription starting at $9 fits your budget, you want to keep future migration cheap.
verdict_b: Choose Batch if your project is transactional + marketing push with behavioral segmentation SDK, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.

faq.a: Airship uses subscription from $9, and Batch uses undisclosed pricing with free tier. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern. Batch offers a free tier; Airship does not.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Airship to Batch?
faq.a: Our data puts Airship at low lock-in (audiences and channels bound to airship data model), and Batch at medium lock-in (push/messaging sdk integration). Moving from Airship to Batch should be manageable, though you'll still need to replay integrations and re-test flows end-to-end.
Both score 4/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis. Batch does edge ahead on pricing/docs transparency (4/5 vs 2/5), which can make evaluation faster.

faq.q: Is Batch a good alternative to Airship?
faq.a: Batch is a reasonable alternative to Airship when your workload leans more toward european mobile apps wanting GDPR-native push with segmentation than mobile. The pricing model shifts too — Airship is subscription from $9, Batch is undisclosed pricing with free tier — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: Batch is explicitly not for teams needing email, SMS, and in-app in one mature suite, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
